originalTitle: Voting suspended in parts of Ethiopia over security concerns
summary: Prime Minister Abiy Ahmed's party is expected to dominate the election as conflicts overshadow the vote.
content:
Voting in Monday's general election was suspended in parts of Ethiopia's Oromia and Amhara regions due to security concerns, but long voter queues were seen elsewhere, electoral commission head Melatwork Hailu announced.

Conflict in parts of Africa's second most populous country had already meant that many would not be able to take part in the seventh election since the end of the Marxist military regime in 1991.

In fact, the whole northern region of Tigray, which is trying to recover from a civil war that ended in 2022, has been totally excluded from the poll.

Overall, while more than 50,000 polling stations were operational, 143 failed to open over security issues.

Casting his vote, Prime Minister Abiy Ahmed, who has been in power since 2018, praised the country's progress since the end of the military dictatorship.

"The Ethiopian people have demonstrated that they do not need anyone to advise or lecture them in order to build their state and establish a democratic system," he said.

The media is tightly regulated in Ethiopia and many organisations, including the BBC, have not been given press accreditation.

Kenya's former President Uhuru Kenyatta, who is observing the poll for the African Union, told reporters that voting was progressing smoothly, the Reuters news agency said.

Abiy, although not directly elected, is likely to come out on top again along with his Prosperity Party. Voters elect representatives to the 547-member parliament and the party that secures at least 274 seats earns the right to form the next government to lead the country for the next five years.

Abiy, 49, came to power following widespread anti-government protests against the Ethiopian People's Revolutionary Democratic Front (EPRDF), a coalition - dominated by politicians from Tigray - that had ruled since 1991.

He went on to dissolve the EPRDF, of which he was a part, and replace it with the Prosperity Party, a more centralised and less federal form of governing.

Prof Merera Gurdina, a veteran opposition politician and member of the Oromo Federalist Congress, alleges the upcoming election is the least competitive in Ethiopia's recent history.

"We are participating symbolically because the law says you cannot boycott elections consecutively. We are participating, mainly to avoid deregistration," he told the BBC.

When Abiy first assumed office, he was hailed as a champion of democracy and press freedom after releasing hundreds of politicians and journalists from prison.

He was awarded the 2019 Nobel Peace Prize mainly for bringing an end to the 20-year military stalemate with neighbouring Eritrea, but Ethiopia's relations with its northern neighbour are once again dangerously fraught.

Seven years later things could not be more different.

Critics accuse his government of suppressing dissent, forcing opponents into exile and arresting political rivals.

Under Abiy's watch the government went to war in 2020 with Tigray's leaders in a two-year conflict estimated by the African Union's mediator to have killed some 600,000 people and which drove the region to the precipice of famine.

According to Reporters Without Borders' 2025 press freedom index, Ethiopia came 148 out of 180 countries.

In its September 2025 publication, Human Rights Watch condemned Ethiopia's government for arbitrarily arresting journalists and media professionals and called for an end to the harassment of independent journalists.

After the credentials for three reporters working for the Reuters news agency were revoked in February, the Committee to Protect Journalists said there was "a troubling pattern of repressive regulatory action against international and independent press in Ethiopia".

What do the prime minister's supporters say?

They argue that Abiy has transformed the country for the better.

The capital, Addis Ababa, symbolises these reforms as it is undergoing a rapid urban transformation, in what is known as the prime minister's "Corridor Development" and "Riverside" projects.

Though these initiatives to improve transport and public spaces have too faced criticism for mass demolitions that have displaced tens of thousands of residents.

Abiy's economic reforms have received support from the International Monetary Fund and the World Bank, under the proviso the country liberalises its foreign currency exchange market and gets a handle on its debt. This stood at $36.5bn (£27.1bn) in 2024, according to the World Bank.

With a population of 135.9 million, Ethiopia is Africa's second most-populous nation - after Nigeria - and one of the continent's fast-growing economies, according to World Bank estimates.

This is despite the ongoing insecurity in regions such as Amhara and Oromia, the hangover from the Tigray war and the rising costs of goods and services.

The country's GDP per capita - which is a measure of a person's average income - is projected to reach $1,133 in 2026, compared to $641 in 2016.

Besides continuing tensions in Tigray, two of the country's most-populous regions, Amhara and Oromia, have experienced violent insurgencies in recent years.

Fano militias in Amhara and the Oromo Liberation Army (OLA) in Oromia continue to fight government forces, resulting in thousands of civilian deaths and forcing hundreds of thousands of people from their homes.

Both want greater ethnic autonomy and feel betrayed by Abiy, though for differing reasons:

According to conflict monitoring group Acled, more than 9,400 people were killed in 2024 as a result of the violence in those regions.

Despite the insecurity, the government says 97% of areas in Amhara and Oromia are ready to hold elections.

The Coalition for Ethiopian Unity, an opposition alliance formed to contest the elections as a unified front, disputes this.

"We have previously stated that we cannot campaign in the Amhara and Oromia regions because there are no enabling conditions," Mistreselasie Tamrat, the coalition's secretary, told BBC Amharic.

Magnus Taylor
chineseTitle: 出于安全考虑，埃塞俄比亚部分地区暂停投票
chineseSummary: 由于冲突给投票带来了影响，预计总理阿比·艾哈迈德的政党将在选举中占据主导地位。
chineseContent:
选举委员会主席梅拉特沃克·海鲁宣布，出于安全考虑，周一大选的投票在埃塞俄比亚奥罗米亚州和阿姆哈拉州部分地区暂停，但其他地方选民却排起了长队。

这个非洲第二人口大国部分地区的冲突已经意味着许多人将无法参加自 1991 年马克思主义军事政权结束以来的第七次选举。

事实上，正在努力从 2022 年结束的内战中恢复过来的整个提格雷北部地区已被完全排除在民意调查之外。

总体而言，虽然有超过 50,000 个投票站正在运行，但有 143 个投票站因安全问题而未能开放。

自2018年起执政的总理阿比·艾哈迈德在投票时赞扬了该国自结束军事独裁统治以来所取得的进步。

“埃塞俄比亚人民已经证明，他们不需要任何人的建议或教训来建设自己的国家和建立民主制度，”他说。

埃塞俄比亚的媒体受到严格监管，包括 BBC 在内的许多组织都没有获得新闻认证。

据路透社报道，正在观察非洲联盟投票结果的肯尼亚前总统乌胡鲁·肯雅塔告诉记者，投票进展顺利。

阿比虽然不是直接选举产生的，但很可能与他的繁荣党一起再次脱颖而出。选民选出由 547 名议员组成的议会的代表，获得至少 274 个席位的政党将有权组建下一届政府，在未来五年内领导该国。

49 岁的阿比在针对埃塞俄比亚人民革命民主阵线 (EPRDF) 的广泛反政府抗议活动后上台，该联盟由提格雷政客主导，自 1991 年以来一直执政。

他随后解散了他所属的EPRDF，并以繁荣党取而代之，这是一种更集中、更少联邦的治理形式。

资深反对派政治家、奥罗莫联邦党国会议员梅雷拉·古迪纳教授声称，即将举行的选举是埃塞俄比亚近代历史上竞争最不激烈的一次。

“我们象征性地参与，因为法律规定你不能连续抵制选举。我们参与，主要是为了避免注销，”他告诉英国广播公司。

阿比刚上任时，在释放了数百名政客和记者后，被誉为民主和新闻自由的捍卫者。

他被授予 2019 年诺贝尔和平奖，主要是因为结束了与邻国厄立特里亚长达 20 年的军事僵局，但埃塞俄比亚与其北方邻国的关系再次陷入危险的困境。

七年后，情况已经完全不同了。

批评者指责他的政府镇压异议、迫使反对者流亡并逮捕政治对手。

在阿比的领导下，政府于 2020 年与提格雷领导人展开了为期两年的战争，非洲联盟调解人估计，这场冲突已造成约 60 万人死亡，并将该地区推向饥荒的边缘。

根据无国界记者组织的 2025 年新闻自由指数，埃塞俄比亚在 180 个国家中排名第 148 位。

人权观察在 2025 年 9 月的出版物中谴责埃塞俄比亚政府任意逮捕记者和媒体专业人士，并呼吁停止骚扰独立记者。

路透社通讯社三名记者的记者资格于二月份被吊销后，保护记者委员会表示，“埃塞俄比亚存在着一种令人不安的针对国际和独立媒体的压制性监管行动”。

首相的支持者怎么说？

他们认为阿比让这个国家变得更好。

首都亚的斯亚贝巴象征着这些改革，因为它正在经历快速的城市转型，即总理的“走廊发展”和“河滨”项目。

尽管这些改善交通和公共空间的举措也因大规模拆除而导致数万名居民流离失所而受到批评。

阿比的经济改革得到了国际货币基金组织和世界银行的支持，条件是该国开放外汇市场并控制债务。据世界银行称，到 2024 年，这一数字将达到 365 亿美元（271 亿英镑）。

据世界银行估计，埃塞俄比亚拥有 1.359 亿人口，是非洲人口第二多的国家（仅次于尼日利亚），也是非洲大陆快速增长的经济体之一。

尽管阿姆哈拉和奥罗米亚等地区持续不安全，提格雷战争的后遗症以及商品和服务成本不断上涨，但情况仍然如此。

该国的人均国内生产总值（衡量一个人的平均收入）预计将在 2026 年达到 1,133 美元，而 2016 年为 641 美元。

除了提格雷的持续紧张局势外，该国人口最多的两个地区阿姆哈拉和奥罗米亚近年来也经历了暴力叛乱。

阿姆哈拉的法诺民兵和奥罗米亚的奥罗莫解放军（OLA）继续与政府军作战，导致数千平民死亡，迫使数十万人离开家园。

两人都希望获得更大的民族自治权，并感到被阿比背叛，尽管原因不同：

据冲突监测组织 Acled 称，2024 年，这些地区的暴力事件导致 9,400 多人死亡。

尽管存在不安全局势，政府表示阿姆哈拉州和奥罗米亚州 97% 的地区已准备好举行选举。

埃塞俄比亚团结联盟是一个反对派联盟，旨在作为统一战线参加选举，但对此提出了异议。

该联盟秘书米斯特雷拉西·塔姆拉特 (Mistreselasie Tamrat) 告诉 BBC 阿姆哈拉语：“我们之前曾表示，我们无法在阿姆哈拉和奥罗米亚地区开展竞选活动，因为没有有利条件。”

马格纳斯·泰勒
whyItMatters: 这条信息属于全球新闻信号，适合用于观察外部环境、政策变化、地缘风险和市场情绪。
